Until this time, the Canaanites had defended themselves, but here they planned to attack Israel. Their minds were confused, and their hearts hardened toward their destruction. Although often at odds with each other, they united against Israel. Oh that Israel would learn from the Canaanites to sacrifice private interests for the public good, and to put aside all disagreements among themselves so that they may unite against the enemies of God's kingdom!

Other people heard this news and were driven by it to make war upon Israel; but the Gibeonites were led to make peace with them. Thus, the discovery of the glory and grace of God in the gospel is for some a fragrance of life leading to life, but for others a fragrance of death leading to death. The same sun softens wax and hardens clay. The falsehood of the Gibeonites cannot be justified. We must not do evil that good may come; we have reason to think Joshua would have been directed by the oracle of God to spare their lives. But once they had said, “We have come from a far country,” they were led to say it was made of skins and their clothes: one lie brings on another, and then a third, and so on. The way of that sin is especially downhill. Yet their faith and prudence are to be commended. In submitting to Israel, they submitted to the God of Israel, which implied forsaking their idolatries. And how can we do better than cast ourselves upon the mercy of a God of all goodness? The way to avoid judgment is to meet it through repentance. Let us do like these Gibeonites, seek peace with God in humility and godly sorrow; so our sin will not be our ruin. Let us be servants to Jesus, our blessed Joshua, and we shall live.

The Israelites, after examining the provisions of the Gibeonites, quickly concluded that they confirmed their account. We hurry more than is good when we do not take God with us, and do not consult him through the word and prayer. The fraud was soon discovered. A lying tongue lasts only for a moment. Had the oath been unlawful in itself, it would not have been binding; for no obligation can make it our duty to commit sin. But it was not unlawful to spare the Canaanites who submitted, abandoned idolatry, and desired only that their lives might be spared. A citizen of Zion swears to his own hurt and does not change his mind. Joshua and the princes, when they found that they had been deceived, did not go to Eleazar the high priest to be released from their commitment, much less did they pretend that no faith is to be kept with those to whom they had sworn. Let this convince us how we ought to keep our promises and make good our bargains; and what conscience we ought to have regarding our words.

The Gibeonites do not justify their lie, but plead that they did it to save their lives. And the fear was not merely of the power of people; one might flee from that to Divine protection; but of the power of God himself, which they saw engaged against them. Joshua sentences them to perpetual bondage. They must be servants, but any work becomes honorable when it is done for the house of the Lord and its offices. Let us, in like manner, submit to our Lord Jesus, saying, We are in your hand; do unto us as seems good and right unto you, only save our souls; and we shall not regret it. If He appoints us to bear his cross and serve him, that will be neither shame nor grief to us, while the lowest office in God’s service will entitle us to a dwelling in the house of the Lord all the days of our life. And when we come to the Savior, we do not proceed on a chance. We are invited to draw near, and are assured that he who comes to Him, He will by no means cast out. Even those things which sound harsh, and are humbling, and form sharp trials of our sincerity, will prove of real advantage.
